ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper proposes a clear, novel meta-clustering pipeline (WATSET) that combines local graph-based word sense induction per node with a subsequent global hard clustering on a disambiguated sense graph — a non-trivial architectural combination not present in the listed baselines (CW, MCL, MaxMax, ECO, CPM). While the components (graph construction from synonym dictionaries, embedding-weighting, local WSI, global clustering) are individually well-studied, their particular composition and application to synset induction appears to be a notable and well-differentiated contribution rather than a mere rehash. From my perspective as a theory-oriented reviewer, the idea is sufficiently original to inform experts, though it would be stronger if accompanied by formal problem framing or theoretical guarantees for the meta-clustering step. | B(4): The paper proposes a new meta-clustering pipeline (WATSET) that combines local graph-based word sense induction and contextual disambiguation with a global hard-clustering step to produce synsets — an approach that is not a straightforward reuse of any single existing algorithm. Framing synset induction as sense-aware meta-clustering and integrating synonym dictionaries with distributional weighting is a non-trivial methodological advance compared to the listed baselines (CW, MCL, MaxMax, ECO, CPM). The authors clearly separate their contributions from prior work and demonstrate cross-lingual empirical gains on English and Russian, which would be informative to practitioners. Given these points, the work is notably novel though it builds on established components rather than introducing wholly unprecedented theory.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper proposes a clear, novel meta-clustering pipeline (WATSET) that combines local graph-based word sense induction per node with a subsequent global hard clustering on a disambiguated sense graph — a non-trivial architectural combination not present in the listed baselines (CW, MCL, MaxMax, ECO, CPM). While the components (graph construction from synonym dictionaries, embedding-weighting, local WSI, global clustering) are individually well-studied, their particular composition and application to synset induction appears to be a notable and well-differentiated contribution rather than a mere rehash. From my perspective as a theory-oriented reviewer, the idea is sufficiently original to inform experts, though it would be stronger if accompanied by formal problem framing or theoretical guarantees for the meta-clustering step.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper proposes a new meta-clustering pipeline (WATSET) that combines local graph-based word sense induction and contextual disambiguation with a global hard-clustering step to produce synsets — an approach that is not a straightforward reuse of any single existing algorithm. Framing synset induction as sense-aware meta-clustering and integrating synonym dictionaries with distributional weighting is a non-trivial methodological advance compared to the listed baselines (CW, MCL, MaxMax, ECO, CPM). The authors clearly separate their contributions from prior work and demonstrate cross-lingual empirical gains on English and Russian, which would be informative to practitioners. Given these points, the work is notably novel though it builds on established components rather than introducing wholly unprecedented theory.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper is generally well organized (intro → method → experiments → conclusion) and states its main contributions early—introducing WATSET and its meta-clustering pipeline—making the overall flow easy to follow. Notation appears consistent and hyperparameters are reported; figures/tables and examples (datasets, baselines, metrics) are informative and support the claims about F-score improvements across English and Russian resources. Writing quality is good with thorough related work coverage, though there are minor presentation issues (e.g., no mention of released code and occasional terse algorithmic description) that prevent a top score under my exacting standards for reproducibility and exposition. | B(4): Overall the paper is well organized and the main contributions are stated early (WATSET meta-clustering for synset induction combining dictionaries and embeddings). Notation and methodology are described clearly with hyperparameters reported and an informative experimental setup across English and Russian gold standards; figures/tables and examples are reported as effective. Minor presentation concerns remain (e.g., code release not mentioned and some discussion of dataset-driven score variability could be expanded), but writing quality and related-work coverage are strong.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper is generally well organized (intro → method → experiments → conclusion) and states its main contributions early—introducing WATSET and its meta-clustering pipeline—making the overall flow easy to follow. Notation appears consistent and hyperparameters are reported; figures/tables and examples (datasets, baselines, metrics) are informative and support the claims about F-score improvements across English and Russian resources. Writing quality is good with thorough related work coverage, though there are minor presentation issues (e.g., no mention of released code and occasional terse algorithmic description) that prevent a top score under my exacting standards for reproducibility and exposition.

Notation and methodology are described clearly with hyperparameters reported and an informative experimental setup across English and Russian gold standards; figures/tables and examples are reported as effective. Minor presentation concerns remain (e.g., code release not mentioned and some discussion of dataset-driven score variability could be expanded), but writing quality and related-work coverage are strong.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: Both reviewers agree the main claim is clear and well supported by empirical comparisons to strong baselines on multiple datasets, indicating the method is technically sound. Deductions come mainly from gaps in experimental rigor (missing statistical significance testing, limited ablation studies, and lack of scalability analysis or code release), but these are relatively minor and do not undermine the validity of the results, so a score of 4 is warranted.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper clearly states its main claim (WATSET meta-clustering improves synset induction) and provides empirical evidence against reasonable baselines on multiple datasets, but the summary shows no formal problem statement or theoretical guarantees for the meta-clustering procedure. Mathematical derivations/proofs are not reported, and I see no mention of complexity analysis or scalability measurements; these are important given the graph-based pipeline. The experimental methodology uses appropriate baselines and multiple datasets, yet the summary lacks rigorous ablation studies (fewer than three controlled removals reported) and no statistical significance testing is mentioned, leaving some uncertainty about which components drive gains and whether improvements are robust.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper's main claims are clearly stated and supported by empirical evidence: WATSET is evaluated against five strong clustering baselines on four gold-standard datasets (English and Russian) and reports higher F-scores, with hyperparameters documented. There are no complex mathematical derivations required and none appear incorrect or missing for the graph-based method. My concerns are limited to experimental rigor: the summary does not mention statistical significance testing or code release for reproducibility, and dependence on input dictionary sparsity is acknowledged but not fully quantified. Overall the technical approach and results are sound with only minor gaps in experimental validation and reproducibility.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper evaluates WATSET against an appropriate set of five established graph-clustering baselines (CW, MCL, MaxMax, ECO, CPM) on four gold-standard datasets for two languages using standard precision/recall/F-score metrics, which indicates a broadly fair empirical setup. However, the summary does not state whether the authors report cases where baselines match or beat WATSET or provide a detailed analysis isolating which components drive improvements; given my emphasis on rigorous comparative analysis, this suggests mostly good comparisons but with minor omissions in explicit comparative discussion and component-level analysis. | B(4): The paper benchmarks WATSET against five strong, relevant graph-clustering baselines (CW, MCL, MaxMax, ECO, CPM) on multiple gold standards in English and Russian using the same precision/recall/F-score metrics, which is appropriate and largely fair. Hyperparameters are reported and the methodology differences (local sense induction + global clustering) are explained and situated in a thorough related-work section. It is not fully clear from the summary whether the authors explicitly acknowledge cases where prior methods match or exceed WATSET or whether any important non-graph baselines were omitted, so I deduct one point for these minor but important omissions in comparative transparency.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper evaluates WATSET against an appropriate set of five established graph-clustering baselines (CW, MCL, MaxMax, ECO, CPM) on four gold-standard datasets for two languages using standard precision/recall/F-score metrics, which indicates a broadly fair empirical setup. However, the summary does not state whether the authors report cases where baselines match or beat WATSET or provide a detailed analysis isolating which components drive improvements; given my emphasis on rigorous comparative analysis, this suggests mostly good comparisons but with minor omissions in explicit comparative discussion and component-level analysis.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper benchmarks WATSET against five strong, relevant graph-clustering baselines (CW, MCL, MaxMax, ECO, CPM) on multiple gold standards in English and Russian using the same precision/recall/F-score metrics, which is appropriate and largely fair. Hyperparameters are reported and the methodology differences (local sense induction + global clustering) are explained and situated in a thorough related-work section. It is not fully clear from the summary whether the authors explicitly acknowledge cases where prior methods match or exceed WATSET or whether any important non-graph baselines were omitted, so I deduct one point for these minor but important omissions in comparative transparency.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper tackles a core NLP problem with a strong, practical contribution: a novel WATSET method, multilingual evaluation, and comparisons to multiple solid baselines. Reviewer A’s concerns about the absence of formal problem framing, theoretical guarantees/complexity analysis, and more rigorous ablation/significance testing justify withholding a perfect score, so a 4 reflects the paper’s strong empirical relevance while acknowledging these methodological gaps.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper addresses a core NLP problem (synset induction) and presents a novel graph-based meta-clustering method (WATSET) with thorough empirical comparisons across four gold standards and five strong baselines, which makes it a good fit for a top NLP/ML venue. However, from the provided summary I see no formal problem statement, theoretical guarantees or complexity analysis, and no mention of extensive controlled ablations or statistical significance testing — omissions that a theory-oriented audience will penalize. Overall the contribution and evaluation are strong and relevant, but the lack of formal/analytical treatment and rigorous ablation/significance evidence prevents a perfect fit.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper addresses a core NLP problem (synset induction) with a method (WATSET) that combines symbolic dictionaries and distributional embeddings — exactly the kind of practical, resource-focused work valued at top NLP/ML venues. It evaluates against five strong graph-clustering baselines using standard precision/recall/F-score metrics on four gold standards across English and Russian, reports hyperparameters and related work, and discusses realistic limitations (dictionary sparsity). Given the multilingual evaluation, strong baselines, and engineering clarity, it is an excellent fit for a top conference audience.

IMPACT.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper makes a solid, practical contribution by improving synset induction across languages and datasets and by offering a clear, reproducible pipeline that practitioners can adopt. While it is not theoretically groundbreaking and depends on input dictionary coverage (and code availability), the empirical gains and cross-lingual validation justify a moderately high impact score of 4.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper tackles the well-motivated task of automatic synset induction and empirically improves over five strong graph-clustering baselines across English and Russian resources, which makes it a useful practical contribution. However, from the provided summary the approach appears as an engineering-style meta-clustering pipeline without formal problem characterization or guarantees, which limits its potential to change theoretical perspectives in lexical semantics. It nevertheless offers a pragmatic recipe (combining dictionary graphs, embeddings, and WSI) that other practitioners and resource builders will likely adopt and extend, opening moderate new applied research directions.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper tackles the important practical problem of automatic synset induction and does so with a well-engineered, hybrid approach (combining synonymy dictionaries and distributional embeddings) that materially improves F-score over five strong graph-clustering baselines on multiple gold standards for both English and Russian. The empirical gains across languages and datasets, together with reported hyperparameters and a clear pipeline (local sense induction + global clustering), make the method likely to change how practitioners build lexical resources and to be adopted or extended in downstream systems. It is not fully transformative because reliance on input dictionary coverage and the absence of an explicit code release (not mentioned) limit immediate broad uptake, but it opens meaningful directions for integrating symbolic resources with embeddings for lexical induction.

SUBSTANCE.justification: Both reviewers agree the empirical evaluation is strong—comparison to multiple baselines across several gold datasets, reported metrics and hyperparameters, and a clear discussion of limitations and related work. They also agree important analyses are missing (ablation/sensitivity studies, runtime/statistical testing) and code release is not mentioned, which prevents a top score. Given the solid experimental backbone but lacking analyses for deeper insight and full reproducibility, a score of 4 is appropriate.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper provides a reasonably comprehensive experimental comparison against five strong baselines across four gold-standard datasets and reports precision/recall/F-scores, and it documents hyperparameters and discusses a key limitation (sensitivity to dictionary sparsity). However, the summary contains no indication of controlled ablation studies isolating the contributions of core components (sense induction, embedding-weighting, disambiguation, final clustering), nor of runtime/complexity analysis or statistical significance testing. Related work coverage is described as thorough, but reproducibility is unclear since code release is not mentioned. From my perspective as a theory-focused reviewer, these missing analyses prevent a higher score.

reviewer_b.justification: The experimental evaluation is strong: WATSET is compared against five established clustering baselines across four gold-standard datasets in two languages, with precision/recall/F reported and hyperparameters reported. The paper documents limitations (dictionary sparsity, cross-resource variability) and situates itself well in related work. However, the summary does not mention released code or explicit ablation/sensitivity studies isolating components (e.g., effect of embedding weighting vs. raw graph), which would be important for full reproducibility and understanding of what drives gains.

main_claims: Proposes WATSET, a new graph-based meta-clustering method to automatically induce synsets from an ambiguous graph of synonyms.
Combines synonymy dictionaries and distributional word embeddings, applies word sense induction to disambiguate nodes, and then globally clusters the disambiguated sense graph to produce synsets.
WATSET outperforms five state-of-the-art graph clustering methods (CW, MCL, MaxMax, ECO, CPM) in terms of F-score on four gold-standard datasets for English and Russian.
_summary.methodology: Build a weighted synonymy graph from dictionary sources (optionally weighted by cosine similarity of word embeddings), perform graph-based word sense induction on each node to produce sense clusters, disambiguate neighbour words by matching sense contexts, construct a disambiguated sense graph, and apply a hard clustering algorithm to induce final synsets.
limitations: Dependence on completeness/sparseness of the input synonymy dictionary; missing edges/bridges can reduce synset recall and lead to smaller-than-desired synsets.
Cross-resource variability: different gold standards (e.g., RuThes vs YARN) lead to different absolute scores due to coverage/domain differences.

reviewer_a.name: Theory Purist
reviewer_a.background: Senior computational linguist with a PhD in formal semantics and 12+ years reviewing experience on ACL/EMNLP program committees; focuses on theoretical foundations of lexical semantics and formal guarantees for algorithms.
traits: Penalises papers that introduce heuristic pipelines without formal definitions or provable properties (deducts points if no theoretical guarantees or formal problem statement).
Requires clear complexity analysis and scalability measurements (deducts points if runtime/space complexity and empirical scaling are not reported).
Demands rigorous ablation studies that explain which components produce gains (deducts points if fewer than 3 controlled ablations).
Insists on statistical significance testing for reported improvements (deducts points if no significance/variance or cross-validation is provided).
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: I focus on whether the paper provides formal problem characterization, algorithmic guarantees, and rigorous analyses that explain why the method works, and I penalize work that is primarily an engineering stack of heuristics without these elements.

reviewer_b.name: Practical Empiricist
reviewer_b.background: Applied NLP researcher and industry scientist with 10+ years building lexical resources and word-sense-aware systems; experienced reviewer for CoNLL and ACL tracks valuing empirical advance and reproducibility.
traits: Rewards clear empirical improvement over strong baselines on realistic datasets (adds points when multiple baselines are beaten consistently).
Favors methods that combine symbolic resources with distributional representations for practical gains (raises score if approach integrates dictionaries and embeddings).
Values multilingual evaluation and applicability (raises score if experiments cover multiple languages or resource regimes).
Prefers reproducibility and engineering clarity (raises score when implementation details and parameter settings are documented or released).
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: I prioritize novel, well-engineered methods that demonstrably improve real-world tasks across languages and that are reproducible, even if they are heuristic rather than theoretically proven.
